prompt: Which statement best captures what it means to do vibe coding *right* (directing an AI to write code well rather than recklessly)?
choices: Accept whatever the AI generates as long as the code runs and looks right
Let the AI commit and merge its own changes to save time
Write the largest possible prompt so the AI produces the whole feature in one go
Start from clear intent, work in small reviewable steps, and read and understand every diff before accepting it
explanation: Doing vibe coding well means staying in control: clear intent plus small, reviewed steps lets you catch errors early. Accepting code just because it runs ignores that AI output can be plausible-but-broken.

prompt: Select ALL of the following that are disciplines of doing vibe coding well.
choices: Ship code you don't fully understand as long as it compiles
Keep secrets and sensitive data out of prompts
Read and understand the AI's output before accepting it
Use tests and version control as a safety net, committing working states often
explanation: Reading the output, leaning on tests and version control, and keeping secrets out of prompts each guard against a real risk. Shipping code you don't understand is the opposite of a discipline, since compiling says nothing about correctness.

prompt: While vibe coding, the AI proposes a fix that calls `db.fetchAllFast()` — a method that does not exist in your library — and after you point it out it keeps re-suggesting slight variants of the same non-existent call. What is happening, and what should you do?
choices: The model is just having an off day; paste the output anyway
It is hallucinating an API and looping; stop prompting, take the wheel, and write the call yourself
The library is broken; commit the generated code to force a fix later
Nothing is wrong; if it compiles in your editor it must be correct
explanation: Inventing a non-existent method and repeating it is a hallucination loop, and the fastest fix is to take over and write the real call yourself. Pasting it anyway or committing the broken code just spreads the error instead of resolving it.

prompt: A QA/Tester is asked to sign off on a feature a teammate vibe-coded by pasting a large unread block of AI output. Which approach reflects doing this right? Select ALL that apply.
choices: Treat the AI-written code as untrusted until it is read and tested
Review the diff for hard-coded secrets and missing auth checks before approving
Run the tests (or write them) so plausible-but-broken logic surfaces before merge
Approve it because the app starts up without crashing
explanation: Treating the code as untrusted, testing it, and reviewing for secrets and missing auth all catch defects before merge. The app starting without crashing proves nothing, since silent wrong code still runs.

prompt: While vibe coding, the AI is about 80% of the way to a fix but keeps looping on the last 20%, re-suggesting variants. Which *trade-off* between continuing and intervening is right?
choices: Accept the 80% version and move on, since most of it works
Take the wheel and finish the hard 20% yourself — the looping cost now exceeds just writing it
Restart the whole task from a blank prompt to reset its 'mood'
Keep prompting; with enough tries the model always converges
explanation: Once the model loops, the time spent re-prompting outweighs the effort of finishing the hard part yourself, so taking the wheel is the right trade-off. Accepting the 80% version ships an incomplete fix, and the model is not guaranteed to converge.
